yeah speaking of home base when we were there uh in the early days of our new office like new setup in the office we rearranged some things we put in one of these cameras and it was like way too aggressive and for the people that were remote it was quite the experience when we were in like the bigger like just big table a lot of people around just constantly just zooming in like almost like so funny it's like always such a weird thing when that happens um yeah how was your time there i feel like it's always hard like i feel like on the marketing side some of the best growth marketers i know like stay places like like 10 to 18 months and then a lot of good

I mean, a lot of positive things.

And so, you know, injecting that into the business had a lot of different interesting challenges of trying to just things foundationally that you may usually do earlier on and easy to do, easier to do early on are a bit harder to establish.

But, you know, it's a great culture, great team, you know, had planned on being there certainly longer, but was so, you know, I was kind of at this point in my career where I was thinking about things a little bit different.
And they have, do I want to continue going ahead of growth rule?
You know, a lot of times.

Just being at those styles of companies, I was usually like the go-to for the CMO, worked very closely with them, and that being the shortest tenure of the C-suite oftentimes came with some interesting ups and downs.

And so, you know, the way I viewed it of everything that was changing with AI, where my skill sets and interests were, I had been working with Marcel a bit, and, you know, as a customer first, and I was like, I think this is a pretty unique opportunity for me to, you know, challenge myself in some new ways and lean into some areas where I'm not getting to flex as much on the typical day-to-day.
And so, we're continuing to lean into that here, you know.
Got it.
Got it.
it.

Yeah, and tell me, if you don't mind, I'm happy to share more about what I'm looking to learn and talk about, but tell me a little bit more about your role at GrowthX, because, like, I never, I never know what to make of, like, titles on the, I don't know if you guys refer to yourselves as an agency, but,
Just tell me about what you do there.
Yeah, I mean, it's continually shifting, to be honest.
So like a little bit on like, you know, my career is I started agency side, then I went in-house for a while.

And so I got my early start in the early days of digital marketing, got to work with a lot of amazing brands, you know, built a lot of experience around paid and testing and analytics, really wanted to broaden my skills.
And so that was part of what brought me in-house and found I had a lot of success along the way, like growing organic programs.
So that became like a bit of like, okay, here's someone who has like a diverse set of channels experience and has built organic engines.

And so just so many brands are like stuck spending way too much on paid search and they need to figure out how to get off of that and or not off of it, but how to, you know, bring in other channels into the mix.
And so because of that background, when I joined GrowthX, I had the mindset of like, okay, because of my agency experience, I can.
And help us out in all sorts of different ways.
Yeah, we don't look at ourselves as a conventional agency, but to a lot of our customers, we probably look and feel like an agency in a lot of ways.
So first, I was leading the client services side of the business.
I built the delivery org.
I onboarded the vast majority of our customers.
Then there was a bit of a need on the sales side of the house of how do we get beyond just pure founder-led, relying on Marcel all the time.
And so I jumped into that mix and spent a, I think I jumped in April or something to that effect.
And up until just literally three weeks ago, we just hired our first founding AE.
He's amazing.
He's now getting up to speed.
He's doing super, super well.
Like, I'm super excited to see what he does.
And so now that he's taken on some of that capacity of, you know, from me running first calls to closing deals, et cetera, it just enables me to go into other areas where I can help the business.
So I'm doing the combination of helping out with early stage accounts, like you all were in the strategy sprint, for example.
And making sure some of those are in a good place and we're building the right strategy.
similarly on like the, you know, delivery side of the org, as we call it, you know, similar mindset, but just those that are out of the sprint phase.

So, yeah, I'm kind of like a Swiss army knife that, you know, just gets thrown at different things of like what needs fixing kind of, and I'll help the best I can.

I'm still certainly learning more and more, understand, you know, the focus on small, medium businesses.
I can see some maybe parallels from, like, a home-based perspective, perhaps, there.
And, you know, helping them out through that critical point of, like, managing taxes and all that comes with it.
And, you know, I would imagine kind of functioning as their CPA to some degree or another.

What I think I am less understanding of at this point in time is, like, you know, the use cases that make you uniquely positioned in a compelling way that's going to, like, resonate and be your differentiator.

I think, like, broadly I understand the space you're in.
It's that connection point to, like, okay, yes, I get it of how you are coming to market and the pain point and where this is going to resonate.
So how do I think about that?
Like a marketing standpoint.
Okay.
Amazing.
This is going to be the best conversation because this is basically what I wanted to talk with you about.
So my role here at town is head of business and it's basically like a grab back for everything business.
We have a great engineering product team and you've probably met Tony or in Slack channels with him and he's a designer as well.
So he's just like a, he's definitely, he's like a Swiss army knife of a human being for sure.
So I think what we so far have been really great at and are over-rotated on is like, you know, great founding engineering.

I think Tony calls it Irish chic, which makes me laugh every time.

And this is why I wanted to speak with you.

I think what we've done with GrowthX so far, and I don't know, maybe there are other conversations you're having with Tony and Jean-Bee in the background about this.

Like as we go into like post-October 15th after this first set of filing deadlines.
And then especially.
January, like when things kind of take off for tax.

So the thing I'm particularly eager to get your perspective on, and I'm asking a bunch of different fractional folks, I know this question as well, but I think you have a unique perspective and also fractional folks always tend to be like pitching whenever it is they're pitching, which I totally get and respect.
Like I respect the hustle, truly I do, and the viewpoints are valuable.

If we were to come to you tomorrow or, like, you know, be looking at, hey, how do we put together a demand strategy that's rooted in content and can pull levers like social, paid social, SEO, like podcast PR, you know, like all of the above, like, it seems like we would need to have some answer to those questions.
What I'm struggling with a little bit is, like, I don't think there's a desire to do a full navel-gazing product marketing exercise.

So is there a world in which, or like, given that situation and other entities you've seen, like, that may or may not have been in this position in the past, like, is it possible to run, like, a directed process where we're answering some of these questions in a structured amount of time to get to, like, outputs that we've used to inform demand campaigns?
That's, like, that's kind of what I'm turning around in my mind.
And that's a lot to share with you all at once.
But I think you're kind of, you have.
A unique background to kind of help us think through this.
So that's what I'm hoping to think through with you.
Yeah, very interesting.
It's a lot to unpack with that.
Yes, yes, it is.
Sorry, I just like talked very fast to you.
good.
I like these sorts of things.
I'm just trying to process a little bit.
So how many customers do you have today?
don't, I didn't get a sense of that today.

gonna

like, how does that uniquely connect and position to your product in some way?

okay.

But like, how can you get people on your payroll product?

And great, how do we lean into that?

Like I've never been at those nodes and connection points.

Or, like, ready-to-go campaign messaging.

Some of it comes through, certainly, with what we did during the strategy sprint for you all.

And I think it's partly a symptom of them being pulled in multiple different directions.

And so typically, though, it is around, like, that that should be a good partner in helping me understand how these people tick, you know, what motivates them, who we care about, who we don't care about.
What kind of, like, informed, strong hypotheses we have in terms of messaging.

And oftentimes that is, like, tends to be, like, a few kind of, like, perspectives on, like, here's our short one-liner on these important topical things associated to, like, I'm to think of an example.
Like, when I was at Trey Leading Growth, one of the things we were trying to figure out was how to get in front of a less technical audience and get them to build on Trey.
It actually turned out that I was, like, overly optimistic and, like, that wasn't probably the ideal angle, but that was the hypothesis at the time was, like, how can we democratize automation?

And so, you know, as I thought about who that persona is, who that, like, what orgs they sit in and how we want to communicate that, you know, we are helping the less technical do technical-based things.
Like, what is that statement?
Like, what do we feel good about we place our stake on?
about on?
Just to think about it of, like, you know, what is, like, the boundaries of, like, what I have, I can play with, like, we're aligned that we need to at least say it in this way.
Okay, that, that gives me enough, like, leverage and context to, like, play around the boundaries of that in different ways as I'm, like, doing campaigns and experimenting.
So I looked at it as, like, I guess, like a jumping off point and helping me just, like, figure out what to focus on and, you know, what that dynamic of coordinating those campaigns looked like.
Like, it was, I'm going say, quite different company to company.
Like, so many marketing differently.
I was at Anaplan, product marketing rolled up into the product org, which was quite weird.

And then we're doing a lot of, like, sales enablement-based things.
And so it was, like, a
lot of my work with product marketing tended to be more like integrated-based campaigns of like bigger point-in-time efforts that we're doing.
I see.

Yeah, that makes sense.

Is that a part of the...
Growthx process.

So basically, think of the strategy sprint as something we run as like an eight-week start to our engagements, and the reason we do this is we have learned that as best as possible, gaining deep knowledge and context and then aligning with our customers on how that context looks and feels on paper, and then reviewing the outputs as we leverage that context, is how we get to more repeatable outputs from AI.
We can move really, really fast, but the better we can make sure we're aligned on these key points, it allows us to get to more repeatable, higher quality.

outcomes, essentially, and so it's something we've just learned with a lot of experimentation across, like, you know, the different things between people, process, and technology, like how they have to come together to allow us to, you know, lean into what we do best, and so part of what we did here was create these different artifacts, and so, not sure if this is, you've followed me along here, but, yeah, so, like, here's an example, like, what we have in terms of audience personas, a bit of a different format of how I've seen this in, in some of my past stops, but, like, you know, some of the best product marketing orgs I've been at is they have these different, like, persona archetypes, and then we're really going into, like, what really motivates these people, what demotivates them, et cetera, and so, yeah, you know, I view this as, like, this helps educate our team at a deeper level, it helps us extract as much knowledge from your team as possible, and then all the different back and forth of your feedback and continuing to help us improve this, allows us to take this and make

And go deeper because, you know, these are the sorts of things our team is continuing to leverage.
And it's easy to like do this and then just say like it's done.
Really the goal is that these should be like things we can come back to over time.
Your business is going to change.
How does that get reflected here?
Or your perspective changes maybe.
But anyways, point being is like some of that same stuff is like what we've baked into this.
Now that's, you know, this is more from a content generation standpoint.
But I still think a lot of transferable knowledge.
No, for sure.
It's great to know this exists, by the way.
I was like clicking on the exact same things you were at the same time.
So that's awesome.
Thanks for directing me there.
I think that a lot of this is great and certainly is a jumping off point.
Like, I mean, the personas are really helpful.
I think the competition one is the one that's got me twitching a little bit from what I'm looking through.
Just because like I don't know that these are the right competitors.
And that may also be driving the like, hey.
How do we confirm that we're thinking about this correctly to kind of speak to, and this is a future state, right?
We haven't done this together yet.
Like the blog content doesn't really address this issue at all.
Like it's just like a different set of, it's like a different body of work.
But I think the direct competitor piece is something that maybe we rethink.
That would be helpful.
I mean, the way we think about this essentially, I mean, there's different ways we can, of course, measure against them, which is, you know, want to make sure we're measuring it's the right folks.
The other thing that we'll be doing is like continually assessing for just different like signals that could inspire what we focus on from maybe a topical standpoint.
That's not the only way we do that, but it is a useful way of like monitoring for those things.
So at times, even if they're like less like super direct, that can still be helpful.
But what is important for the team to understand is the nuance of like why some.
Or bubbling up in being a true direct and what are those traits that make them that?
Because that could certainly take shape of what we choose to prioritize and why.
Yeah, that's a good point.
Okay, let me think about that a bit more.
Well, this is really helpful.
I appreciate you just reaching out and making the connection with me.
I don't know exactly where we'll take things beyond where we are today.
But I think we'll definitely have kind of more needs beyond the blog content as we grow.
So, yeah, just appreciate it.
Yeah, happy to chat again.
You know, any thoughts, questions, feel free to send my way.
I think the more I continue to get up to speed, the more useful I also can be in that.
And so, also happy to share ideas as I get more context of where y'all are focused.
And so, but anyways, I'm looking forward to it.
It should be fun.
Awesome.
Awesome.
Thank you, Kyle.

All right.
Sounds good.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Discuss Town's marketing strategy and potential next steps with GrowthX.ai](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [Town lacks clear product marketing foundations (personas, competitive analysis, differentiated value prop)](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [There's a need to balance partner-led acquisition with direct customer growth](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [GrowthX's strategy sprint process could provide valuable insights for marketing efforts](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=0.0)
  - [Kyle's experience suggests focusing on specific customer segments (e.g., "starters") can drive growth](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=981.0)

## Topics

### Town's Current Marketing Position

  - [Few hundred customers, mainly through partnerships](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=689.0)
  - [Lack of classic product marketing elements (personas, competitive analysis, value prop)](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=548.0)
  - [Strong engineering and design, but marketing strategy needs development](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=499.0)
  - [Blog content created with GrowthX, but more strategic approach needed](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=506.0)

### GrowthX's Role and Capabilities

  - [Kyle's role: Swiss army knife, adapting to various needs (client services, sales, strategy)](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=336.0)
  - [Strategy sprint process: 8-week engagement to gain deep knowledge and context](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=1339.0)
  - [Creates artifacts like audience personas and company context documents](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=1369.0)
  - [These documents inform AI-driven content creation and marketing strategies](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=1444.0)

### Marketing Strategy Considerations

  - [Need to identify key customer segments and their motivations](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=765.0)
  - [Focus on "trigger events" that lead businesses to need Town's solution](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=787.0)
  - [Potential to become a trusted source for specific customer segments (e.g., new business owners)](https://fathom.video/share/Zvx4ha3GMGEPdgXPFtTczqHfas2fkiDR?tab=summary&timestamp=981.0)
